content:
Extinction-level capitalism a citizenвҖҷs thoughts
on AI risk

AI is inherВӯently politВӯical techВӯnology. If AI works as intended, it will gradВӯuВӯally corrode our liberal democВӯracy, risking an irreВӯversible shift into another politВӯical and economic configВӯuВӯraВӯtion. Among AI risks, this one deserves more considВӯerВӯaВӯtion, because it requires no addiВӯtional condiВӯtions like malign actors or AI malfuncВӯtion. AI only needs to amplify existing trends, espeВӯcially around concenВӯtraВӯtion of capital. This damage will occur even assuming that in the near term, AI will broadly improve mateВӯrial well-being.

About MB

IвҖҷm a self-employed author, designer, programmer, and lawyer. In 2022, I learned that my own works were in the training datasets of generВӯaВӯtive-AI compaВӯnies. In response, I invented the first set of lawsuits chalВӯlenging the legality of these pracВӯtices. IвҖҷm currently co-counsel for plainВӯtiffs in a number of AI cases. Though I discuss certain legal issues below, I am not your lawyer, and nothing here is held out as legal advice. These are my personal views as a citizen and economic actor; I speak only for myself. This piece is typeset in Equity, AdvoВӯcate, and TripВӯliВӯcate, fonts I designed. They can be licensed for your own polemics and pamphlets.

Emergent effects

Two billion years ago, the rock layers comprising what is now called the Colorado Plateau began to form: first igneous and metaВӯmorВӯphic rocks, followed by many layers of sediВӯmenВӯtary rocks. About fifty million years ago, through tectonic action, this plateau gained thouВӯsands of feet of elevaВӯtion. About five million years ago, a river began to flow. The river carried silt and debris, scraping out the beginВӯnings of a canyon. The river deepВӯened the canyon, exposing its walls to weather and erosional forces that widened the canyon further. Today the waterway is the Colorado River. The geologВӯical formaВӯtion is the Grand Canyon.

The formaВӯtion of the Grand Canyon required zero human agency. Zero techВӯnology. Zero coorВӯdiВӯnaВӯtion among the river, the land, and gravity. In that sense the Grand Canyon is an emerВӯgent effect: a complex, unforeВӯseeВӯable output arising from simpler inputs.

But we would never wonder whether the river is sentient. Or whether the river cares about the dirt that it carries out of the canyon. The water is just doing what water does: flowing downВӯhill. The dirt just happens to be in the way.

Inherently political technology

Langdon Winner is a politВӯical theoВӯrist. Winner wrote the excelВӯlent and influВӯenВӯtial essay вҖңDo ArtiВӯfacts Have PoliВӯtics?вҖқ (1980). Winner sought to debunk the tradiВӯtional framing that вҖңtechВӯnoloВӯgies are вҖҰ neutral tools that can be used well or poorly, for good, evil, or someВӯthing in between.вҖқ Instead, Winner proposes two ways that a techВӯnology can affect its politВӯical enviВӯronВӯment:

  1. The techВӯnology is designed to have certain politВӯical effects. For example, the Great FireВӯwall of China, a bundle of techВӯnoВӯlogВӯical measures that limit Chinese citiВӯzensвҖҷ access to foreign inforВӯmaВӯtion sources. AntipoВӯdally, the Tor Project intends to maxiВӯmize user anonymity and thwart governВӯment intruВӯsion.

  2. The techВӯnology is inherВӯently politВӯical. This is WinnerвҖҷs key analytic fulcrum. Winner describes two versions of inherВӯently politВӯical techВӯnology. The first is where the techВӯnology вҖңactuВӯally requires вҖҰ a particВӯular set of social condiВӯtions as [its] operВӯating enviВӯronВӯment.вҖқ For instance, nuclear weapons: the only responВӯsible way to possess such dangerous techВӯnology is to place it within вҖңa centralВӯized, rigidly hierВӯarВӯchical chain of command вҖҰ the [atom] bomb must be authorВӯiВӯtarian; there is no other way.вҖқ The second version is where the techВӯnology is вҖңstrongly compatВӯibleвҖқ with a certain politВӯical arrangeВӯment (even if not strictly required) and thus tends to bring that arrangeВӯment to fruition.

As an example, Winner considers the mechanВӯical tomato harvester. DevelВӯoped at UC Davis in the 1950s, the machine was tremenВӯdously producВӯtive. But it was also expenВӯsive. Only well-capiВӯtalВӯized tomato growers could afford it. The rest couldnвҖҷt compete. According to Winner, the number of CaliВӯfornia tomato growers dropped from ~4000 in the early 1960s to ~600 in 1973, costing ~32,000 jobs and the compounding negaВӯtive effects on those commuВӯniВӯties. Winner summaВӯrizes:

What we see here вҖҰ is an ongoing social process in which scienВӯtific knowlВӯedge, techВӯnoВӯlogВӯical invenВӯtion, and corpoВӯrate profit reinВӯforce each other in deeply entrenched patterns that bear the unmisВӯtakВӯable stamp of politВӯical and economic power вҖҰ oppoВӯnents of innoВӯvaВӯtions like the tomato harvester are made to seem вҖңantitechВӯnologyвҖқ or вҖңantiprogressвҖқ. For the harvester is not merely the symbol of a social order that rewards some while punishing others; it is in a true sense an embodВӯiВӯment of that order.

Not merely the symbolвҖ”the embodВӯiВӯment. A facially neutral techВӯnoВӯlogВӯical invenВӯtionвҖ”here, a tomato harvesterвҖ”can induce politВӯical effects. Those effects donвҖҷt arise from flaws in the techВӯnology. To the contraryвҖ”they arise from its effiВӯcacy.

How are the politВӯical effects deterВӯmined? Winner idenВӯtiВӯfies two key early deciВӯsions. The first is the binary quesВӯtion of whether to pursue the techВӯnology at all. The second are choices about вҖңthe design or arrangeВӯmentвҖқ of the techВӯnology. Winner cautions: вҖң[t]o see the matter solely in terms of cost-cutting, effiВӯciency, or the modernВӯizaВӯtion of equipВӯment is to miss a deciВӯsive elementвҖқ. That is, the politВӯical effects can possibly be counВӯtered, but first they must be acknowlВӯedged.

Of course, the best opporВӯtuВӯnity to choose wisely is before the techВӯnology is widely introВӯduced, as capital and social investВӯment tends to entrench it:

Because choices tend to become strongly fixed in mateВӯrial equipВӯment, economic investВӯment, and social habit, the origВӯinal flexВӯiВӯbility vanishes for all pracВӯtical purposes once the initial commitВӯments are made. In that sense techВӯnoВӯlogВӯical innoВӯvaВӯtions are similar to legislaВӯtive acts or politВӯical foundВӯings that estabВӯlish a frameВӯwork for public order that will endure over many generВӯaВӯtions. вҖҰ The issues that divide or unite people in society are settled not only in the instiВӯtuВӯtions and pracВӯtices of poliВӯtics proper, but also, and less obviВӯously, in tangible arrangeВӯments of steel and concrete, wires and tranВӯsisВӯtors, nuts and bolts.

TechВӯnoВӯlogВӯical choices bear directly on the вҖңpublic orderвҖқ at large. When we donвҖҷt take those choices seriВӯouslyвҖ”or weвҖҷre persuaded to ignore them by those insisting that techВӯnology is just a neutral toolвҖ”we risk politВӯical conseВӯquences.

Winner warns of complaВӯcency. Once the techВӯnology arrives and becomes entrenched, the converВӯsaВӯtion gets reframed as one of techВӯnoВӯlogВӯical inevitabilism vs. anachroВӯnism, and dissent is discourВӯaged: вҖңthe kinds of reasoning that justify the adapВӯtaВӯtion of social life to techВӯnical requireВӯments pop up as sponВӯtaВӯneously as flowers in the spring вҖҰ After a certain point, those who cannot accept the hard requireВӯments and imperВӯaВӯtives will be dismissed as dreamers and fools.вҖқ

Liberal democracy

The balance of power of democВӯracy is premised on the average person having leverage through creating economic value. If thatвҖҷs not present, I think things become kind of scary.
вҖ”a certain AI CEO

Liberal democВӯracy is the politВӯical scienВӯtistвҖҷs term for the type of governВӯment prevaВӯlent among capiВӯtalist economies since the AmerВӯican and French RevoВӯluВӯtions. The intelВӯlecВӯtual founВӯdaВӯtion of liberal democВӯracy arose during the EnlightВӯenВӯment, espeВӯcially through the work of John Locke. Liberal democВӯracy emphaВӯsizes limited governВӯment, indiВӯvidual rights, and sepaВӯraВӯtion of powersвҖ”in short, majority rule with excepВӯtions and guardrails. (The term liberal democВӯracy doesnвҖҷt connote liberals or DemocВӯrats in the specific US politВӯical sense. But politВӯical parties of differing ideoloВӯgies are a tradiВӯtional feature of liberal democВӯraВӯcies.) Today, most liberal democВӯraВӯcies are in Europe, the AmerВӯicas, and the Pacific Rim.

Liberal democВӯracy is not a fixed set of immutable charВӯacВӯterВӯisВӯtics, but a bundle of graded values. All liberal democВӯraВӯcies emphaВӯsize certain ones over others. In the aggreВӯgate, some of these nations evolve toward stronger liberВӯalism; others evolve away. These degraded cases have someВӯtimes been called illibВӯeral democВӯracy: the observВӯable formalВӯiВӯties of liberal democВӯracy may still be observedвҖ”e.g. multiВӯparty elecВӯtions, sepaВӯraВӯtion of powersвҖ”but the lived reality is single-party rule and declining indiВӯvidual rights.

ThatвҖҷs not to say that liberal democВӯracy produces excelВӯlent outcomes for all citiВӯzens, all the time. It doesnвҖҷt. At any moment, certain citiВӯzens are dissatВӯisВӯfiedвҖ”say, because they belong to a group whose rights are inadВӯeВӯquately protected or economВӯiВӯcally marginВӯalВӯized. Liberal democВӯracy offers a process, not a result: grassВӯroots demoВӯcВӯratic particВӯiВӯpaВӯtion can coalesce into policy change. But within the arena of competing politВӯical interВӯests, winners and losers necesВӯsarily follow. NaviВӯgating these differВӯences within a stable, accomВӯmodaВӯtive frameВӯwork is preferВӯable to a rigid one that buckles under these stressesвҖ”say, through politВӯical revoВӯluВӯtion, which tends to be messy and unpreВӯdictable.

CapiВӯtalism has tradiВӯtionВӯally been considВӯered a necesВӯsary but not suffiВӯcient condiВӯtion for liberal democВӯracy. Why? A reguВӯlated market economy encourВӯages citizen particВӯiВӯpaВӯtion through propВӯerty ownerВӯship and transВӯacВӯtion. A prinВӯcipal funcВӯtion of governВӯment is to define the economic condiВӯtions of the state. Economic particВӯiВӯpaВӯtion mutuВӯally reinВӯforces demoВӯcВӯratic particВӯiВӯpaВӯtion. PropВӯerty owners will vote for those who protect their interВӯests. The rise of indusВӯtrial capiВӯtalism in the 19th century, and the wealth-redisВӯtriВӯbВӯuВӯtion mechВӯaВӯnisms that followed in the 20th, led to economic empowВӯerВӯment for more citiВӯzens, and ultiВӯmately broader politВӯical empowВӯerВӯment. The converse also holds: economies premised on state or oligarchic control of some narrow class of assets havenвҖҷt tended to evolve toward liberal democВӯracy.

In pracВӯtice, certain people in a capiВӯtalist liberal democВӯracy tend to get increasВӯingly rich. Absent counВӯterВӯmeaВӯsures, the wealthy gain control of the politВӯical appaВӯratus, thwarting liberal-demoВӯcВӯratic norms. This tension between capital and poliВӯtics is a long-considВӯered topic. A key early work was, of course, Karl MarxвҖҷs Capital (about which more later). In the current era, Mancur OlsonвҖҷs book The Rise and Decline of Nations set out how small groups with a shared interest (which could include capital concenВӯtraВӯtion) can effecВӯtively underВӯmine stable sociВӯeties. More recently, econВӯoВӯmists Robert Reich (вҖңHow CapiВӯtalism is Killing DemocВӯracyвҖқ), James Galbraith (The Predator State), and Yanis VaroВӯufakis (TechВӯnofeuВӯdalism: What Killed CapiВӯtalism) are among those who have studied the escaВӯlating politВӯical conseВӯquences of rising wealth inequality. The synthesis might be: as more wealth becomes concenВӯtrated in the hands of fewer citiВӯzens, liberal democВӯracy weakens, because whichever citiВӯzens are losing economic releВӯvance will also lose politВӯical releВӯvance. A nation sending many of its citiВӯzens toward economic irrelВӯeВӯvance risks becoming politВӯiВӯcally illibВӯeral.

The Skynet fallacy

AI discourse often invokes sci-fi narraВӯtives. IвҖҷve called this the Skynet fallacy, after the TermiВӯnator antagВӯoВӯnist, the most cited. But any sci-fi will do. For instance, one AI CEO warned of AI вҖңgoing TermiВӯnatorвҖқ; Stephen Hawking and other scienВӯtists warned of AI вҖңdevelВӯoping weapons we cannot even underВӯstandвҖқ; a second AI CEO said we вҖңdonвҖҷt need much imagВӯiВӯnaВӯtion [about AI risk] because we grew up with that in the mediaвҖқ; a third AI CEO invoked the sci-fi movies Contact and 2001: A Space Odyssey in a piece about AI risk; a US congressman summaВӯrized AI risk as вҖңevil robots rising up to take over the worldвҖқ; a well-known jourВӯnalist advoВӯcated for more TermiВӯnator analoВӯgies; a promiВӯnent AI-risk pundit said heвҖҷs вҖңannoyedвҖқ with TermiВӯnator analoВӯgies yet has suggested that AI will eradВӯiВӯcate humanity using hordes of toxic nanobots.

ArtisВӯtiВӯcally, sci-fi movies exterВӯnalize the awe and unease of techВӯnoВӯlogВӯical confrontaВӯtion. ItвҖҷs easy to see why these metaphors have become part of AI-risk discourse. And yet. As AI puts down roots in our economy, the sci-fi framing hides more than it reveals. Sci-fi plots are optiВӯmized for cineВӯmatic impact. So as a metaphor for AI risk, they can lead to faulty intuВӯitions. Among realВӯistic AI risks, we can expect that most will be boring, slow, and depend on minimal extra techВӯnology. Whether AI will cause literal human extincВӯtion is esotericвҖ”a lightВӯning strike. But AI could easily induce future economic and politВӯical condiВӯtions that most AmerВӯiВӯcans today would consider intolВӯerВӯableвҖ”a cancer that extinВӯguishes a certain way of life. NobodyвҖҷs going to make a movie about boring AI risks. But they comprise the majority of worriВӯsome AI outcomes.

In 2003, philosoВӯpher Nick Bostrom proposed his now well-known parable of the paperВӯclip maxiВӯmizer. Bostrom imagВӯines an advanced AI that is asked to make paperВӯclips. Taking its mission seriВӯously, the AI eradВӯiВӯcates humanity as it consumes all Earth resources to make more paperВӯclips. Bostrom was illusВӯtrating the AI control problem: ensuring an AI acts consisВӯtently with human priorВӯiВӯties is diffiВӯcult, even for ostenВӯsibly simple goals. Because when we say вҖңAI, make paperВӯclipsвҖқ, the implied coda is вҖңвҖҰ without killing everyone.вҖқ But an AI canвҖҷt know this ex nihilo. Bostrom softens the sci-fi flavor by choosing paperВӯclips and not, say, laser-wielding robots. BostromвҖҷs choice of an economic mechВӯaВӯnism of resource converВӯsion is apt. Even a mundane objecВӯtive can produce outsize risk. We could further observe that on the paperВӯclip-maxiВӯmizing path, human life would become dystopic long before literal extincВӯtion. As resources are depleted, humans would become tenants in a neofeuВӯdalist paperВӯclip empire. (PaperВӯclip Crisis: The Saga BeginsвҖ”opening soon.)

Computer scienВӯtist Stuart Russell also explored the control problem in his book Human CompatВӯible. Russell calls one variant the gorilla problem: that вҖңancesВӯtors of the modern gorilla created вҖҰ the genetic lineage leading to modern humans. How do the gorillas feel about this? вҖҰ the consensus opinion would be very negaВӯtive indeed.вҖқ Despite shared lineage, gorillas and humans have incomВӯmenВӯsuВӯrable values. Nothing humans can doвҖ”short of disapВӯpearingвҖ”would restore gorillas to their golden age. RussellвҖҷs framing is a believВӯable analogy for the future relaВӯtionВӯship of humans and AI. Sure, gorillas didnвҖҷt delibВӯerВӯately invent humans. But I take Russell to mean that the emerВӯgent charВӯacВӯterВӯisВӯtics of these relaВӯtionВӯships are more conseВӯquenВӯtial than the intended ones. (In that sense, Russell echoes Langdon Winner.) The fact that weвҖҷre inventing AI doesnвҖҷt mean we will predict or control its gravest effects. Any more than gorillas could predict or control human domiВӯnance of their ecosystem. The gorillas did their thing. We did ours.

AI will do its thing too. It will take time to figure out what that is, exactly.

How the West was won

I recently read Cadillac Desert by Marc Reisner, about the develВӯopВӯment of water resources in the western US between 1910 and 1980, espeВӯcially the dam-building campaign of the federal Bureau of ReclaВӯmaВӯtion. ReisnerвҖҷs book weaves several storyВӯlines:

EngiВӯneering and enviВӯronВӯmental impacts. Dams concenВӯtrate water. But they cause enviВӯronВӯmental conseВӯquences elseВӯwhere. FurtherВӯmore, ReclaВӯmaВӯtionвҖҷs 20th-century projects were often based on optiВӯmistic projecВӯtions of meteВӯoВӯroВӯlogВӯical water supply. Today, long-term drought condiВӯtions chalВӯlenge those projecВӯtions. No amount of money or hydroВӯlogic engiВӯneering can change that.

National US poliВӯtics. In the early 20th century, newer Western states sought politВӯical clout in the federal governВӯment, which had been domiВӯnated by Eastern states. PolitВӯical clout followed economic growth, and to achieve growth, the Western states depended on one critВӯical but scarce input: water. In the West, the federal Bureau of ReclaВӯmaВӯtion was tasked with increasing water supplies. A politВӯical symbiosis emerged: congressmen from Western states voted to fund ReclaВӯmaВӯtion water projects; in turn, ReclaВӯmaВӯtion looked out for their constituents. Over decades, Reisner depicts this relaВӯtionВӯship as metasВӯtaВӯsizing from pracВӯtiВӯcality to corrupВӯtion, in the sense of ReclaВӯmaВӯtion becoming beholden to a narrow politВӯical lane. In that sense, ReclaВӯmaВӯtionвҖҷs dams arguably qualify as inherВӯently politВӯical techВӯnology.

State economies. ReclaВӯmaВӯtionвҖҷs highly subsiВӯdized water bootВӯstrapped Western economies, espeВӯcially agriВӯculВӯture. For a while, it worked as promised: Farmers got irriВӯgaВӯtion. Cities got water. Western states grew and prosВӯpered. But the projects worked so well that Western states wanted more. These states never weaned themВӯselves from subsiВӯdized federal water, setting them on a path toward permaВӯnent depenВӯdence.

The parallel between water and AI is inexact. Water is a biologВӯical necesВӯsity; AI is not. ReclaВӯmaВӯtionвҖҷs projects worked (up to a point); AI may or may not. This is part of why AI propoВӯnents have sought to raise the stakes. So far, AI has been grueВӯsomely expenВӯsive and delivВӯered middling results. NeverВӯtheВӯless itвҖҷs routinely depicted as a geopoВӯlitВӯical fulcrum, a proxy for continВӯuing US excepВӯtionВӯalism. If AmerВӯiВӯcans donвҖҷt adopt AI wholeВӯheartВӯedly, we will be losers. Do you want to be a loser?

Labor replacement

Q: What is Big AI primarily selling? A: Labor replaceВӯment, with mass unemВӯployВӯment as a likely conseВӯquence. Some disagree or call it doomerish. Why? ItвҖҷs exactly what AI grandees have been telling us. A certain AI CEO wrote that AI вҖңwill be hugely destaВӯbiВӯlizing for hundreds of millionsвҖқ and that AI tools вҖңare fundaВӯmenВӯtally labor replacingвҖқ. A certain AI company released a research paper about вҖңthe labor market impact potenВӯtial of large language modelsвҖқ. That AI CEO said вҖңjobs are defiВӯnitely going to go away, full stopвҖқ. A second AI CEO said that in the near future, вҖң20% of people donвҖҷt have jobs.вҖқ A third AI CEO predicted that farther out, вҖңprobВӯably none of us will have a job.вҖқ An AI-adjaВӯcent CEO said that AI вҖңwill destroy humanВӯiВӯties jobsвҖқ. The ball is not hidden.

Capital markets are already pricing in these expecВӯtaВӯtions. RegardВӯless of whether Big AI evenВӯtuВӯally delivers mass labor replaceВӯment, today these compaВӯnies seek to concenВӯtrate capital as if they will. According to the WashВӯington Post, AI capital expenВӯdiВӯture in 2026 is estiВӯmated to be $700 billion, a вҖңspending spree [that] has few preceВӯdentsвҖқ. Based on a recent survey of US workers, the Global PartВӯnerВӯship on ArtiВӯfiВӯcial IntelВӯliВӯgence said that вҖңthe policy window to shape how AI transВӯforms work is probВӯably closing faster than most governВӯments realize.вҖқ

ExtraВӯorВӯdiВӯnary investВӯment demands extraВӯorВӯdiВӯnary returns. In early 2026, after a certain billionВӯaire tech CEO laid off 40% of his employees and attribВӯuted the deciВӯsion to a new вҖңcore thesisвҖқ of AI, the companyвҖҷs stock rose nearly 25%. He wonвҖҷt be the last. Whether these layoffs are based on actual AI beneВӯfits or merely вҖңanticВӯiВӯpaВӯtoryвҖқ is neither here nor there. Employers have strong incenВӯtives to reduce headВӯcount and increase AI spending before competiВӯtors do. We will increasВӯingly see both kinds of layoffs. SoftВӯware programВӯmers are one set of conseВӯquenВӯtial, highly paid writers likely to be replaced with AI. ElseВӯwhere IвҖҷve predicted that legal pracВӯtice will also be seriВӯously impaired. Why? Like programВӯmers, lawyers write about conseВӯquenВӯtial things and charge a lot. (We can expect that AI compaВӯnies themВӯselves, fond of dogfooding, will perforВӯmaВӯtively set an example through their own AI-driven layoffs.)

Two common objecВӯtions to this concluВӯsion:

  1. вҖңAI wonвҖҷt replace workers, it will enhance them.вҖқ That may turn out to be true. But for now, itвҖҷs not what AI CEOs are pitching. And itвҖҷs not how the market is valuing Big AI. AI customers will natuВӯrally pay less for a worker-assisВӯtive techВӯnology than techВӯnology that reduces headВӯcount, which is one of the biggest expenses at any employer. The labor-replaceВӯment story is everyВӯthing to everyВӯbody. If AI is just a neat way of, say, finding photos of your dog on your phone, none of the AI bets pay out.

  2. вҖңAI will replace certain jobs, but also create new ones.вҖқ Fair, but вҖңcreate new onesвҖқ is skimpy on details. How do the jobs created compare to the ones lost? Do they pay more? Or less, as the supply of workers seeking work increases? Do the new jobs require different skills? Who bears the cost of the career switch? None of this is autoВӯmatic. As econВӯoВӯmist Carl Benedikt Frey said: вҖңMost econВӯoВӯmists will acknowlВӯedge that techВӯnoВӯlogВӯical progress can cause some adjustВӯment probВӯlems in the short run. What is rarely noted is that the short run can be a lifeВӯtime.вҖқ

After going hard on this narraВӯtive for several years, AI CEOs are now softВӯening their tone, primarily in response to public backВӯlash. For instance, the AI CEO who once said вҖңjobs are defiВӯnitely going to go awayвҖқ now wants us to know that watching AI answer his emails felt вҖңdehuВӯmanВӯizingвҖқ and that he now values вҖңthe human part of the rolesвҖқ. ThatвҖҷs what it finally tookвҖ”using your own product? Ed Zitron has charВӯacВӯterВӯized the AI market so far as a blinkВӯered converВӯsaВӯtion between AI execВӯuВӯtives and trad execВӯuВӯtives, all of whom вҖңhave little idea what work looks likeвҖқ. This is more a feature than a bug. To replace labor, AI doesnвҖҷt need to actuВӯally deliver compaВӯrable outputs. It only needs to fulfill a certain execВӯuВӯtive fantasy of вҖңwhat work looks likeвҖқ. ThatвҖҷs much easier than, say, achieving AGI (however defined). On that view, AI may turn out to be a Veblen goodвҖ”an item that buyers value primarily for signaling their ability to afford it.

One more small matterвҖ”funding of the US governВӯment is premised on employВӯment. In 2023, the US got 48% of its revenue from income taxes and 36% from other payroll taxes. If AI causes 30% unemВӯployВӯment, revenues will drop and expenses will jump. Of course, higher unemВӯployВӯment also leads to lower aggreВӯgate consumer spending. These would be fiscal condiВӯtions unknown since the Great DepresВӯsion, and utterly foreign to AmerВӯiВӯcans living today.

InterВӯmisВӯsion

On its current trajecВӯtory, IвҖҷm personВӯally skepВӯtical that AI can deliver economic returns. Over time, I expect that AI will evolve toward a bifurВӯcated market:

  1. Consumer AI. Today, consumers have cheap, high-volume access to fronВӯtier models for, e.g., retouching dog selfies and writing dating profiles. This is unsusВӯtainВӯable and thereВӯfore must end. I expect these uses will increasВӯingly be handled by on-device models that are either free (perhaps open-source) or built into the OS. The only compaВӯnies that can be profВӯitable in this cateВӯgory will be those who control a device ecosystem and can subsiВӯdize model develВӯopВӯment through indiВӯrect revenue streams.

  2. Labor-replacing AI services. These will offer models with substanВӯtially higher complexity and value, but at substanВӯtially higher price. I expect most market compeВӯtiВӯtion will be in this second cateВӯgory. Though itвҖҷs not yet apparent that this cateВӯgory will discover any workВӯable customerвҖ“product equiВӯlibВӯrium. For customers, tokens have to be cheaper than the wages being replaced. For AI suppliers, tokens will have to be wildly profВӯitable, or these compaВӯnies will have to find addiВӯtional ways of extracting rents from customers. However it evolves, I expect this market will be smaller than todayвҖҷs dataВӯcenter investors foresee, and they will follow the path of many fiber-optic investors of the 1990s: into bankВӯruptcy court.

The goodies economy

Big AIвҖҷs message to potenВӯtial corpoВӯrate customers of AI has centered on labor replaceВӯment. Correctly foreВӯseeing that the workers themВӯselves might take umbrage, Big AI has pitched them a sepaВӯrate story: that workers will enjoy higher stanВӯdards of mateВӯrial well-being under AI.

One billionВӯaire AI CEO predicted вҖңuniversal high incomeвҖқ and that вҖңAI and robots will provide any goods and services that you want.вҖқ Another billionВӯaire AI CEO predicted вҖңuniversal extreme wealth for everyВӯbodyвҖқ. A billionВӯaire AI venture capiВӯtalist вҖңbelieve[s] in making everyone rich, everyВӯthing cheap, and everyВӯthing abunВӯdantвҖқ and that вҖңthe ultiВӯmate result will be that all physВӯical goods become as cheap as pencils.вҖқ A tech billionВӯaire predicted вҖңmore goods and services with less laborвҖқ and вҖңalways-availВӯable, high-quality medical advice.вҖқ Another billionВӯaire AI venture capiВӯtalist predicted вҖңthe abunВӯdance of goods and services вҖҰ will be very, very large. Prices will be very, very, low.вҖқ HopeВӯfully yachts will come down too.

But waitвҖ”will they? In the post-AI economy, will we all have yachts? Of course not. As usual, we should be skepВӯtical of billionВӯaires. EspeВӯcially those suddenly claiming to care about the purchasing power of workers. Are they fibbers or just fools? I think itвҖҷs more subtle: these billionВӯaires are performing an idioВӯsynВӯcratic kind of class empathy, probВӯably at the behest of publiВӯcists. This feeling doesnвҖҷt come natuВӯrally. So, like a cyborg reading a love poem, itвҖҷs stilted. The persisВӯtent lack of detail signals that these billionВӯaires have no idea how, exactly, AI will accomВӯplish this. But the subtext is clear: If tolerВӯating worker purchasing power is what it takes to become a trilВӯlionВӯaire, thatвҖҷs a sacriВӯfice IвҖҷm willing to make.

SomeВӯtimes this theory goes by fancy names like prosВӯperity or abunВӯdance. SomeВӯtimes itвҖҷs linked to specific policy mechВӯaВӯnisms like universal basic income. It all comes back to the same idea. IвҖҷm just going to call this stuff goodies. The theory is that under AI, citiВӯzens are going to get some nice level of goodies.

A few reasons we might be skepВӯtical of AI-goodies theory. The price of any consumer good has a lower bound set by the cost of its inputs (if one is not selling at a loss). But labor is only one input. So even if AI reduces labor costs to $0, the cost of other inputsвҖ”e.g., energy and raw mateВӯrialsвҖ”remain. Even if AI lowers commodity prices by increasing their supply (say, through better discovery and extracВӯtion techВӯniques), they are still rivalВӯrous resources. Not all are availВӯable domesВӯtiВӯcally. Postwar, rising global consumpВӯtion of oil in the West influВӯenced the formaВӯtion of OPEC by future petrostates who sought to capture a share of oil-derived wealth. SimiВӯlarly, in a post-AI US economy, foreign nations with critВӯical mineralsвҖ”e.g., lithium, cobalt, nickelвҖ”may likeВӯwise cartelize them, raising costs and creating a price floor. Related kinds of supply control may also arise domesВӯtiВӯcally. Today, a certain US agriВӯculВӯture company uses patented seeds to exert control over a market of ostenВӯsibly indeВӯpenВӯdent farmers. If AI dramatВӯiВӯcally reduces the costs of manuВӯfacВӯturing and agriВӯculВӯture, Big AI may seek to capture value from the factoВӯries and farms that benefit (say, through a sales royalty). AI-goodies theory implies that Big AI will allow maximum producВӯtion of goods, leading to lowest prices. But the huge finanВӯcial projecВӯtions around AIвҖ”set by Big AI itselfвҖ”may force them to do otherВӯwise. That is, Big AIвҖҷs promises of world-historВӯical goodies for consumers and world-historВӯical profits for shareВӯholders may be incomВӯpatВӯible. If soвҖ”guess who prevails?

Still, to be fair to Big AI, IвҖҷll accept their arguВӯment that citiВӯzens will enjoy higher stanВӯdards of mateВӯrial well-being under AI through goodies. But the goodies must be paid for. Somehow, wealth must be transВӯferred from Big AI to everyone else. These transВӯfers can happen by three basic methods:

  1. Direct. Big AI directly provides goodies to citiВӯzens. As one AI CEO put it, to вҖңcapture much of the worldвҖҷs wealth вҖҰ and then redisВӯtribute this wealth to the peopleвҖқ.

  2. Tax and spend. GovernВӯment taxes Big AI at some high rate, and spends the money on public goodies. But the idea that AmerВӯican AI compaВӯnies would consent to high tax rates to ensure public goodies is inconВӯceivВӯable because it vests more control in governВӯment, which no free-markeВӯteer believes should be so.вҖЎ

  3. Market-based. Big AI dramatВӯiВӯcally reduces the cost of goods and services, which then flow into the market at extremely low prices. The flaw in this method is that AI will reduce prices of certain things more than others. For instance, itвҖҷs easy to see how AI will make streaming movies free; food and healthВӯcare and transВӯportaВӯtion, less easy.

These methods arenвҖҷt mutuВӯally excluВӯsive. I expect the most likely method is the first, where Big AI directly provides the goodies, in the style of Gilded Age company towns. Big AI would avoid governВӯment interВӯvenВӯtion through taxaВӯtion and redisВӯtriВӯbВӯuВӯtion. Big AI would control the goodies and who gets them. Big AI would be clearly underВӯstood as the goodie provider. Assuming the massive profits mateВӯriВӯalize, Big AI could afford to make diverse baskets of goodies that include even things like food and healthВӯcare and transВӯportaВӯtion.

Keep in mind the goodies economy will play out against a backВӯdrop of worsВӯening AI-induced labor-market effectsвҖ”high unemВӯployВӯment, but also wage declines, underВӯemВӯployВӯment, misemВӯployВӯment, and so forth. ThereВӯfore, a side effect of the goodies economy is that whatВӯever we think constiВӯtutes the social safety net will shift from a service of governВӯment to a service of private industry, specifВӯiВӯcally Big AI. One AI CEO shameВӯlessly predicts exactly this outcome: that there is вҖңopporВӯtuВӯnity for AI to be used to help proviВӯsion governВӯment services вҖҰ includ[ing] health services, the DMV, taxes, social secuВӯrity, building code enforceВӯment and so on.вҖқ But donвҖҷt worryвҖ”he concludes that because вҖңpoorly impleВӯmented servicesвҖқ cause вҖңcyniВӯcism about governВӯmentвҖқ, AI interВӯmeВӯdiВӯaВӯtion would вҖңstrengthen[] respect for demoВӯcВӯratic goverВӯnance.вҖқ And to be clearвҖ”by вҖңAIвҖқ, he means вҖңmy privately owned AIвҖқ, and by вҖңopporВӯtuВӯnity вҖҰ to helpвҖқ he means a massive governВӯment contract.

This portends a politВӯical shift. CitiВӯzens once treated governВӯment as a politВӯical bulwark against the encroachВӯments and overВӯreaches of industry. In the goodies economy, citiВӯzens will tend to align with Big AI, since it provides the goodies, which for many citiВӯzens may include basic needs. Under those condiВӯtions, if Big AI has a conflict with governВӯmentвҖ”whose side will citiВӯzens take?

IndusВӯtries have often insuВӯlated themВӯselves against politВӯical conseВӯquences with popular prodВӯucts. Arguably, in the last 25 years, the tech industryвҖ”espeВӯcially smartВӯphone and social-media compaВӯniesвҖ”has beneВӯfited from light reguВӯlaВӯtory scrutiny relaВӯtive to its size. ExamВӯples from the last century include the autoВӯmoВӯtive and fast-food indusВӯtries, which likeВӯwise enjoyed decades of gentle reguВӯlaВӯtion, even though both had serious public-health impacts.

To improve public accepВӯtance, Big AI has so far emphaВӯsized their fun consumer-facing prodВӯucts. These prodВӯuctsвҖ”e.g., generВӯating an image, or writing a story, or riding in a driverВӯless carвҖ”make AI seem harmВӯless: the citizen thinks of their own usage as trivial and fun, and thereВӯfore models the aggreВӯgate effect of AI as a sum of simiВӯlarly trivial indiВӯvidual usages. ConsisВӯtent with this, Big AI has often framed these tools as вҖңdemocВӯraВӯtizingвҖқ (ex. 2, ex. 3) implying that oppoВӯnents of these systems are in some sense anti-democВӯracy (or as Winner predicted, antitechВӯnology and antiprogress).

In the goodies economy, as citiВӯzens lose indeВӯpenВӯdence as economic actors, they will also lose indeВӯpenВӯdence as politВӯical actors. They will be captured by industry. EconВӯoВӯmist Yanis VaroВӯufakis has called the current early stage of this arrangeВӯment вҖңtechВӯnofeuВӯdalismвҖқ, a term suggesting how late capiВӯtalism erodes indiВӯvidual economic agency. Through its proviВӯsion of goodies, Big AI intends to complete this tranВӯsiВӯtion.

An economic histoВӯrian might say that a politВӯical takeover by Big AI is unlikely, citing successful worker-led politВӯical moveВӯments in the US and Europe. If the US reaches 30% AI-induced unemВӯployВӯment, the thinking goes, workers will rise up and demand change through politВӯical mechВӯaВӯnisms of our liberal democВӯracy. ThereвҖҷs reason to doubt these paralВӯlels. These events occurred earlier in the techВӯnoВӯlogВӯical era, when industry and workers were adverВӯsarial yet remained interВӯdeВӯpenВӯdent. CitiВӯzens retained irreВӯducible leverage with both industry (as laborers) and governВӯment (as voters). In the decades after the US Gilded Age, this combiВӯnaВӯtion of economic and elecВӯtoral power was critВӯical to the rising status of workers. But in a future post-AI economy, workers could lose their jobs because of Big AI while remaining depenВӯdent on Big AI for basic needs. How do you bite the hand that feeds? FurtherВӯmore, many previous conflicts between industry and workers were not gentle adjustВӯments, but violent (say, the Luddite moveВӯment of the 1810s, and Gilded Age strikes of the late 1800s) or caused devasВӯtating, permaВӯnent social tranВӯsiВӯtions (say, the 1917 Russian RevoВӯluВӯtion).

Legally, shifting the social safety net from governВӯment to Big AI also means that citizen entiВӯtleВӯment to these goodies will shift from public statuВӯtory and constiВӯtuВӯtional law to private-contract law. Private compaВӯnies can withВӯhold goodies for many reasons the governВӯment cannot. For instance: if you talk shit about the governВӯment, you will still get your unemВӯployВӯment check, because the First AmendВӯment says so. But no such restricВӯtion on Big AI: if you talk shit about them, they will be free to revoke your goodies. CanвҖҷt happen? Already does: credit reports are a private system that largely controls who gets favorВӯable interest rates. Or employer-provided healthВӯcare, a strucВӯturally unnecВӯesВӯsary tradiВӯtion that distorts incenВӯtives for workers by making their continued health continВӯgent on continued employВӯment. Most likely, the politВӯical alignВӯment between citiВӯzens and Big AI will not only be enforced by carrot, but also by stick.

Of course, an industry seeking to induce politВӯical alignВӯment from citiВӯzens would benefit not only from distribВӯuting goodies, but also weakВӯening the governВӯment. In 2023, would you have believed this predicВӯtion? That an AI CEO would endorse a presВӯiВӯdenВӯtial candiВӯdate. That the candiВӯdate would win. That on his first day in office, the new presВӯiВӯdent would install the AI CEO in the governВӯment with broad powers, in pursuit of a nebuВӯlous project of cutting waste and saving money. That ultiВӯmately, this project would save little money and primarily impair governВӯment agenВӯcies designed to protect the vulnerВӯable. That somehow the parts of the governВӯment that provided large contracts for the same AI CEO would remain untouched. To be fair, for centuries US busiВӯnesses have sought to bend US public policy to suit their interВӯests, often with great success. This is a theme of GalbraithвҖҷs book The Predator State. WhatвҖҷs new is the mousВӯtache-twirling flagrancy of the project, and that it was underВӯtaken at the behest of the newly elected presВӯiВӯdent. Some called it a coup, though one premised on abdiВӯcaВӯtion of power rather than seizure.

вҖЎ As of June 2026, reporting has suggested that an AI CEO has proposed to the current US presВӯiВӯdent that the US take an equity stake in the AI CEOвҖҷs company (and maybe others). This arrangeВӯment would be very different from taxaВӯtion. It foreВӯsees a transfer of capital from the US governВӯment to the corpoВӯraВӯtion to buy equity. As a shareВӯholder, the US governВӯment would be entiВӯtled to a share of future profits, which may never exist. It also potenВӯtially creates a conflict of interest: as a Big AI shareВӯholder, would the US govt reguВӯlate or legisВӯlate the AI industry? TaxaВӯtion, by contrast, requires no prepayВӯment by the US governВӯment, and can be assessed on the current activВӯiВӯties of the AI company, regardВӯless of profВӯitability. HistorВӯiВӯcally, the US governВӯment has taken equity stakes in private corpoВӯraВӯtions as an indiВӯrect bailout. Which is maybe the better word for what is being proposed here. Absent this exigency, taxaВӯtion is economВӯiВӯcally and politВӯiВӯcally preferВӯable. This AI CEO may also want to ask his chatbot what happened to the oil industry in Venezuela under ChГЎvez.

Know your enemy

Oh Karl, the world isnвҖҷt fair
It isnвҖҷt and never will be
They tried out your plan
It brought misery instead
If youвҖҷd seen how they worked it
YouвҖҷd be glad you were dead.
вҖ”Randy Newman вҖңThe World IsnвҖҷt FairвҖқ

To idenВӯtify as a Marxist today is rare (at least in the US). More often, itвҖҷs wielded as an anti-capiВӯtalist epithet against someone who endorses socialist or CommuВӯnist methods. TrueвҖ”Marx himself supported these methods. After all, he did write The CommuВӯnist ManiВӯfesto. But shortВӯhand Marxism tends to omit the reasoning that Marx, a philosoВӯpher and histoВӯrian, considВӯered vital. After all, he did write Capital.

Marx saw class struggle as the key instiВӯgator of history. He argued that capiВӯtalism was politВӯiВӯcally unstable, and necesВӯsarily led to worker revolt. MarxвҖҷs theory that economic value origВӯiВӯnates in labor overВӯlaps concepВӯtuВӯally with LockeвҖҷs labor theory of propВӯerty. But to Marx, capiВӯtalism sepaВӯratedвҖ”or using his preferred term, alienВӯatedвҖ”workers from the fruits of that labor.

In pracВӯtice, MarxвҖҷs theoВӯries have had mixed success. But MarxвҖҷs obserВӯvaВӯtions in Capital about the interВӯacВӯtion of workers and machines remain releВӯvant, even though he was writing in the mid-1800s, when the pinnacle of techВӯnology was textile machinery. Looking back on worker uprisВӯings since the 1600sвҖ”including the Luddite moveВӯment that famously protested those textile machinesвҖ”Marx wrote:

It took both time and expeВӯriВӯence before the workpeople learnt to distinВӯguish between machinery and its employВӯment by capital, and to direct their attacks, not against the mateВӯrial instruВӯments of producВӯtion, but against the mode in which they are used.

By protesting the textile machines, the Luddites were missing the bigger picture. The Luddites (and earlier workers) were raging against the literal machineвҖ”the specific new techВӯnology. But the techВӯnology was a symptom, not a cause. Instead, workers needed to rage against the bigger, figuВӯraВӯtive machineвҖ”the extracВӯtive capiВӯtalist system.

MarxвҖҷs obserВӯvaВӯtion has a subtler impliВӯcaВӯtion too. New techВӯnology often holds itself out as the starting point of a narraВӯtive: from now on, everyВӯthing is different. When we consider the techВӯnology alone, that narraВӯtive domiВӯnates. But when we zoom out and consider the historВӯical context, the new techВӯnology becomes the current endpoint of a much longer politВӯical narraВӯtive.

What would Marx say to AI criticsвҖ”social, legal, economic, politВӯicalвҖ”that have arisen so far? Maybe that weвҖҷre missing the bigger picture. That as a human invenВӯtion, AI may be the starting point of a new techВӯnoВӯlogВӯical narraВӯtive. But as an affront to human workers, it continues a long tradiВӯtion of capiВӯtalist techВӯnoloВӯgies, beginВӯning with the IndusВӯtrial RevoВӯluВӯtion (if not earlier).

For their part, the capiВӯtalВӯists who own a new techВӯnology usually prefer to frame it as a starting point. ConveВӯniently, this omits the grubby historВӯical context that might dampen the marketing. Similar sleight of hand animates the snide techieвҖҷs favorite sick burnвҖ”LudditeвҖ”connoting a blockВӯhead irraВӯtionally opposed to techВӯnology. Of course, this usage is delibВӯerВӯately ahisВӯtorВӯicalвҖ”read Brian MerchantвҖҷs Blood in the Machine. Perhaps there should be a companion term for a blockВӯhead irraВӯtionally committed to AI.

These two frames for underВӯstanding new techВӯnology under capiВӯtalism also suggest two correВӯsponding analytic approaches to AI risk:

  1. AI as techВӯnology. On this view, AI is a starting point, and we consider the risks of what it can do that previous techВӯnoloВӯgies could not. This isnвҖҷt wrong, exactly. It works for certain charВӯacВӯterВӯisВӯtics. For instance: I can believe that AI will facilВӯiВӯtate hacking and similar crimВӯiВӯnality like no techВӯnology before. That risk is specific to AI as new techВӯnology and deserves considВӯerВӯaВӯtion.

  2. AI as capiВӯtalist instruВӯment. But for other charВӯacВӯterВӯisВӯticsвҖ”say, AIвҖҷs effects on labor, public wealth, and the economyвҖ”thinking of AI strictly as new techВӯnology embeds the error Marx warns against. For those charВӯacВӯterВӯisВӯtics, we should consider how AI may amplify or accelВӯerate existing trends. Put differВӯentlyвҖ”the ways in which AI is inherВӯently politВӯical techВӯnology.

In sumвҖ”as new techВӯnology, AI acts as a creator of new risks; as a capiВӯtalist instruВӯment, it acts as an ampliВӯfier or accelВӯerВӯator of existing risks and trends in the capiВӯtalist system.

The strongest version of this framing is that the вҖңampliВӯfier or accelВӯerВӯatorвҖқ part is precisely Why AI Exists. UnpreceВӯdented billions have already been poured into AI. Why? Because itвҖҷs expected to deliver more profit and concenВӯtrate more wealth than any previous techВӯnology. But ampliВӯfying these trends also means ampliВӯfying the attenВӯdant politВӯical risks. Earlier I accepted the arguВӯment that Big AI will deliver abunВӯdant goodies to citiВӯzensвҖ”which AI propoВӯnents consider one of the strongest points in its favor. But doing so will likely increase alignВӯment between Big AI and citiВӯzens, with negaВӯtive politВӯical conseВӯquences. As usualвҖ”no free lunch.

This shift in framing also shifts the evidenВӯtiary context. When we think about AI risk, weвҖҷre necesВӯsarily making guesses about the future. But when we frame AI in the narrow sense of new techВӯnology, weвҖҷre primarily considВӯering a timeВӯline that starts now. Whereas when we shift to thinking of AI as a capiВӯtalist instruВӯment, weвҖҷre considВӯering a timeВӯline that starts centuries ago and has evolved continВӯuВӯously into the present. We can and should study those existing economic and politВӯical trends, because those will likely shape the future trajecВӯtory. Put differВӯently: AI may be new. But itвҖҷs not immune to history.

The poisoned chalice

I think the biggest chalВӯlenge to AI in this country is politВӯical unrest вҖҰ Can you make more money? ItвҖҷs all irrelВӯeВӯvant if the country blows up вҖҰ If I were sitting here in private with my peers, IвҖҷd be telling them вҖҰ the country could blow up politВӯiВӯcally. And none of us are going to make any money when the country blows up.
вҖ”an AI-adjaВӯcent CEO

Big AIвҖҷs goal of labor replaceВӯment has two dimenВӯsions someВӯtimes overВӯlooked, although they are both hidden in plain sight. The first relates to AIвҖҷs effort to replace knowlВӯedge workers. The other pertains to the competВӯiВӯtive effects of doing so.

AI replacing knowlВӯedge workers. Labor replaceВӯment is the mechВӯaВӯnism. StrucВӯturally, Big AI seeks to build a moat around US economic growth by normalВӯizing AI as a cogniВӯtive interВӯmeВӯdiary where knowlВӯedge workвҖ”roughly, economВӯiВӯcally valuВӯable thinking and creativityвҖ”will happen.

This maneuver is part of the automaВӯtion playВӯbook, espeВӯcially for the tech industry. During the rise of desktop automaВӯtion in the 1980s and 1990s, tech compaВӯnies sought to build moats by controlВӯling the file formats, programs, and platВӯforms where docuВӯments were created. As the commerВӯcial internet ripened during the 2000s, tech compaВӯnies sought moats based on leverВӯaging network effectsвҖ”espeВӯcially the unholy dyad of adverВӯtising and social media.

How Big AI plans to profit from this interВӯmeВӯdiВӯaВӯtion is an open quesВӯtion. One AI company has suggested taking a cut of AI-assisted discovВӯeries. The logisВӯtics and legalВӯiВӯties would be boggling. DetailsвҖ”whatВӯever. For now, AI compaВӯnies largely agree on the first step: make workers depenВӯdent on AI to do their jobs, just as tech foreВӯbears made workers depenВӯdent on a certain softВӯware program to share a file, or on a certain website to have friends. This time, however, the softВӯware ultiВӯmately consumes the worker.

Big AIвҖҷs timing is canny. In recent decades, intanВӯgible assets have played an increasing role in global economic growth. An intanВӯgible asset lacks physВӯical embodВӯiВӯment, and includes formal intelВӯlecВӯtual-propВӯerty assets (e.g., copyВӯrights, patents) but also informal (e.g., brand goodВӯwill, other knowlВӯedge-work assets). Tangible assets are the oppoВӯsiteвҖ”bricks, mortar, invenВӯtory, and so forth. According to the WIPO, since 1995 global investВӯment in intanВӯgible assets has steadily increased, and since 2009 has exceeded that of tangible assets. By wide margin, the US leads investВӯment in intanВӯgible assets.

For a long time, intanВӯgible assets were underВӯvalued because tradiВӯtional GDP measures and accounting pracВӯtices excluded them. In the 1970s and 1980s, during the first big wave of IT investВӯment, the US expeВӯriВӯenced a counВӯterВӯinВӯtuВӯitive slowВӯdown in producВӯtivity growth, an effect econВӯoВӯmist Erik BrynВӯjolfВӯsson called the вҖңproducВӯtivity paradoxвҖқ. Part of the resoВӯluВӯtion to the producВӯtivity paradox is that returns from IT investВӯment were maniВӯfesting not as labor producВӯtivity per se, but as intanВӯgible assets. For busiВӯnesses, IT investВӯment was worthВӯwhile even if it didnвҖҷt increase labor producВӯtivity, because it still generВӯated intanВӯgible assets. (Another part of the resoВӯluВӯtion to the producВӯtivity paradox is BrynВӯjolfВӯssonвҖҷs theory of lagging compleВӯmenВӯtary investВӯment, which will also likely apply to AI.)

NeverВӯtheВӯless, increasing labor producВӯtivity matters a lot to workers. Why? It has historВӯiВӯcally been the biggest driver of wage growth. Which brings us to anotherвҖ”maybe the mostвҖ”conseВӯquenВӯtial trend in the US economy over the last 50 years: wage stagВӯnaВӯtion, or the decouВӯpling of producВӯtivity and wage growth. As the story is typiВӯcally told: from 1948 to 1973, wage growth (91.3%) basiВӯcally matched producВӯtivity growth (96.7%). Since 1973, however, overall producВӯtivity has grown (74.4%) much faster than wages (9.2%). Where has the money gone instead? Into the pockets of busiВӯness owners, mostly. Over the same timeВӯframe, the operВӯating profits of busiВӯnesses (as measured by net operВӯating surplus) has steadily increased. Today, if wage growth had kept up with producВӯtivity gains since 1973, weвҖҷd expect wages to be more than double.

Together, these trends have been a long-term double whammy for US workers. Because of wage stagВӯnaВӯtion, workers have been generВӯating increasing value for employers (as producВӯtivity) but not capturing via wages the same share of that value they once did. Because of the shift toward intanВӯgible assets, employers have invested less in labor producВӯtivity, further inhibiting wage growth. As AI replaces knowlВӯedge workers, we can expect it to amplify both trends.

Will it? Recent labor-market research by one major AI company suggests that the greatest вҖңtheoВӯretВӯical capaВӯbilityвҖқ for AI labor replaceВӯment lies with tradiВӯtional cateВӯgories of knowlВӯedge work: finance, programВӯming, engiВӯneering, science, legal, and the arts. A research group at Tufts UniverВӯsity was blunter: вҖңthe more AI helps you do your job, the more expendВӯable you can become. Finance profesВӯsionals, teachers/profesВӯsors, creative profesВӯsionals, accounВӯtants and audiВӯtors, legal profesВӯsionalsвҖқ are at risk. Even BrynВӯjolfВӯsson said he was вҖңsurprisedвҖқ that knowlВӯedge work would be so readily replaced.

вҖңTechВӯnology always makes certain jobs obsoВӯlete; new ones will arise.вҖқ AIвҖҷs predicted labor replaceВӯment is unpreceВӯdented in three ways: the diverВӯsity of tasks replaced; its outsize effect on highly educated workers; and the backВӯdrop of 50 years of wage stagВӯnaВӯtion. AutomaВӯtion-driven tranВӯsiВӯtions arenвҖҷt necesВӯsarily easy, even when theyвҖҷre narrow and the economy can absorb the workers. Those who handВӯwave over the details should study historВӯical examВӯples. When you tell a large group of workers that their skills no longer have economic value, you risk a politВӯical and social tinderbox. Recall Carl Benedikt FreyвҖҷs comment: вҖңthe short run can be a lifeВӯtimeвҖқ.

ConfiВӯdenВӯtial to owners of capital: nothing IвҖҷm saying above depends on a normaВӯtive view about wealth distriВӯbuВӯtion. I personВӯally oppose wage stagВӯnaВӯtion and believe workers should enjoy wage growth proporВӯtional (at minimum) to producВӯtivity growth. If you donвҖҷtвҖ”fine. The arguВӯment persists. Even accepting that the AI labor tranВӯsiВӯtion is economВӯiВӯcally rational and necesВӯsary, it will still likely have politВӯiВӯcally destaВӯbiВӯlizing effects that you, owners of capital, will find unpleasant. Plus, youвҖҷll get punched in the wallet tooвҖ”keep reading.

The competВӯiВӯtive effects of replacing knowlВӯedge workers. The previous section considВӯered how AI labor replaceВӯment will affect the relaВӯtionВӯship between workers and employers. But it will also induce more carnivВӯoВӯrous compeВӯtiВӯtion among firms that adopt AI, and thus indiВӯrectly among the owners of capital. Whereas the last 50 years of the US economy have featured wage stagВӯnaВӯtion, the post-AI economy may feature someВӯthing akin to capital stagВӯnaВӯtion, for non-AI cateВӯgories of capital. A growing economy used to be a rising tide that lifted all boats. Post-AI, it may only lift a handful of yachts. Everyone else will be in dry dock. Arguably that capital tranВӯsiВӯtion is already underway.

How might this work? The economy is increasВӯingly driven by intanВӯgible assets. KnowlВӯedge workers provide those assets. If a company lays off its knowlВӯedge workers in favor of adopting some vendorвҖҷs AI, at first it gets the same producВӯtivity at much lower cost. So much win.

But in doing so, the company commodiВӯtizes its own output. If your company can autoВӯmate its output via AI, others can too. Your existing competiВӯtors, certainly. But also upstarts who donвҖҷt have your cost footВӯprint. WhatВӯever intanВӯgible assets AI can generate will be produced in excess, leading to a deflaВӯtionary market for that asset. A companyвҖҷs knowlВӯedge workers may be its greatest expense. But they also contribute to its competВӯiВӯtive moat.

FurtherВӯmore, the ongoing market value of a company is necesВӯsarily tied to its differВӯenВӯtiВӯated assets. A company valued at a billion dollars that adopts AI will then have to build some new asset not replicВӯable with AI, or it wonвҖҷt be worth a billion much longer. EveryВӯthing that makes AI an excelВӯlent cost collapser makes it an equally excelВӯlent capital collapser. A poisoned chalice. The greatest irony will be if AI-adopting compaВӯniesвҖ”having laid off knowlВӯedge workers en masseвҖ”hire them back merely to ensure that competiВӯtors cannot. In recent tech history, there have already been signs of this dynamic.

Consider large law firms, aka Big Law. Currently certain legal-AI starВӯtups license LLMs from Big AI and repackage them for Big Law at high prices. These starВӯtups claim to add other special sauce. OK, sure. WhereвҖҷs the economic equiВӯlibВӯrium? If legal-AI starВӯtups prove that money can be made selling AI to Big LawвҖ”wonвҖҷt Big AI just sell to Big Law directly, and cut out the starВӯtups? Or if legal-AI starВӯtups prove that AI can effecВӯtively provide legal servicesвҖ”wonвҖҷt legal-AI starВӯtups just sell to clients directly, and cut out Big Law? WonвҖҷt members of Big Law that adopt AI have to lay off a lot of equity partВӯners, because adopВӯtion of AI will shrink profit margins? WonвҖҷt the members of Big Law refusing AI have to consolВӯiВӯdate to preserve their margins? Or just cave to AI? (The tendency of competiВӯtors to adopt similar pracВӯtices is called instiВӯtuВӯtional isomorВӯphism.) So it goes. Most states prevent nonlawyers from sharing in legal fees, so law firms will probВӯably remain a distinct set of entiВӯties. But one plauВӯsible equiВӯlibВӯrium is that legal-AI starВӯtups disapВӯpear (quickly), and members of Big Law consolВӯiВӯdate (relaВӯtively quickly) until there are only a handful left, all contracting directly with Big AI.

Along these lines, I expect that to succeed finanВӯcially, Big AI will likely need to demolish a signifВӯiВӯcant number of existing tech compaВӯnies and grab their revenue for itself. By the process described above: Big AI essenВӯtially uses its tech customers as an R&D facility. Big AI licenses models to these compaВӯnies. Tech compaВӯnies compete to adapt their busiВӯnesses to AI. Once a concept is proven, Big AI directly takes over that market. The labor-replaceВӯment story will grow into a company-replaceВӯment story. Many of those tech compaВӯniesвҖ”and their shareВӯholders in the public marketsвҖ”may also find that AI is a poisoned chalice.

The resource curse

The theory is thought also to fit Middle Eastern oil exporters espeВӯcially well. In this region, governВӯmentsвҖҷ access to rents, in the form of oil revenue, may have freed them from the need for taxaВӯtion of their peoples, and that this in turn freed them from the need for democВӯracy.
вҖ”Jeffrey Frankel

Since AI is new, there are not yet examВӯples of post-AI economies. So we might ask a broader quesВӯtion: what happens to nations whose economies are concenВӯtrated around one inherВӯently politВӯical techВӯnology? The most instrucВӯtive compaВӯraВӯbles in the world economy are probВӯably petrostates: nations whose national wealth is concenВӯtrated in oil or natural gas. The analogy is imperВӯfect. Unlike AI, oil is anchored to geogВӯraphy and limited in supply. On the other hand, oil has proven finanВӯcial value; AI does not.

Petrostates are vulnerВӯable to what econВӯoВӯmists call the resource curse, with two distincВӯtive sets of effects:

  1. Capital effects. The concenВӯtrated resource tends to pull labor and capital from the rest of the economy, depriving other economic sectors, espeВӯcially manuВӯfacВӯturing and agriВӯculВӯture. This makes the national economy more depenВӯdent on imports for basic needs, and more sensiВӯtive to price volatility of the resource. If the resource price declines suffiВӯciently, the nation will be unable to keep affording imports, but also unable to fulfill those needs with domestic producВӯtion.

  2. PolitВӯical effects. The value of the concenВӯtrated resource creates what Jeffrey Frankel calls вҖңa politВӯical contest to capture ownerВӯshipвҖқ, which in turn encourВӯages the emerВӯgence of autoВӯcratic or oligarchic instiВӯtuВӯtions captured by an economic elite who seek to retain control of the resource. The process is self-reinВӯforcing in two ways. First: the economic elite uses its wealth to repress politВӯical oppoВӯnents. Second: as the governВӯment derives more income from the concenВӯtrated resource, it relies less on taxaВӯtion of citiВӯzens, which weakens demoВӯcВӯratic accountВӯability.

According to the Council on Foreign RelaВӯtions, вҖңpetrostates include Algeria, Cameroon, Chad, Ecuador, Indonesia, Iran, KazaВӯkhstan, Libya, Mexico, Nigeria, Oman, Qatar, Russia, Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, and Venezuela.вҖқ No one would confuse this with a list of successful liberal democВӯraВӯcies. Due to its increasing oil and gas producВӯtion, some also consider the US a petrostate.

Which petrostates provide the most goodies to citiВӯzens? The Gulf petrostatesвҖ”Qatar, UAE, Saudi Arabia, Oman. These nations distribute petrowealth to citiВӯzens through substanВӯtial public beneВӯfits (e.g., housing, healthВӯcare, educaВӯtion, public-sector jobs) while having low rates of taxaВӯtion. The tradeoff, of course, is that these counВӯtries are not even notionВӯally demoВӯcВӯratic. They are heredВӯiВӯtary monarВӯchies. They offer none of the beneВӯfits that citiВӯzens in a liberal democВӯracy enjoy (or someВӯtimes take for granted)вҖ”demoВӯcВӯratic particВӯiВӯpaВӯtion, indiВӯvidual civil rights, freedom of speech.

But the Gulf states were never liberal democВӯraВӯcies. Can the resource curse send a liberal democВӯracy spiraling into autocВӯracy? SureвҖ”consider Venezuela. Today, Venezuela is an authorВӯiВӯtarian regime. But from 1958 to the 1990s, it was a stable liberal democВӯracy. This changed with the 1998 elecВӯtion of Hugo ChГЎvez. After taking office, ChГЎvez pursued populist economic poliВӯcies funded by national oil wealth. EmboldВӯened by high oil prices, ChГЎvez amended the constiВӯtuВӯtion to bolster his own power and installed loyalВӯists at the top of VenezuelaвҖҷs national oil company (PDVSA). He rediВӯrected oil revenues to a series of domestic social programs (the вҖңBoliВӯvarian missionsвҖқ). ChГЎvez leverВӯaged his resulting popuВӯlarity among workers to consolВӯiВӯdate his power, for instance by ending term limits and taking control of the judiВӯciary. (ConfiВӯdenВӯtial to AI CEOs: think careВӯfully before you offer an ownerВӯship stake to any US presВӯiВӯdent who has authorВӯiВӯtarian and populist tendenВӯcies.) After oil prices dropped in 2014, the resource curse hit Venezuela hard, putting its economy into crisis and allowing ChГЎvezвҖҷs successor NicolГЎs Maduro to estabВӯlish a dictaВӯtorВӯship. DemonВӯstrating a central irony of the resource curse: while the resource is generВӯating wealth and delivВӯering goodies to citiВӯzens, authorВӯiВӯtarian moves are depicted as essenВӯtial to sustaining prosВӯperity. After the economy weakens, authorВӯiВӯtarian moves are depicted as essenВӯtial to restoring prosВӯperity. The ratchet only tightens. The river flows only downВӯhill.

Norway is another outlier. Norway is a robust liberal democВӯracy. After oil was discovВӯered in its North Sea terriВӯtoВӯrial waters in the 1960s, Norway created the state-owned Statoil to build and operate oil facilВӯiВӯties. In 1990, Norway estabВӯlished the GovernВӯment Pension Fund Global, a soverВӯeign-wealth fund to receive surplus oil revenues and invest them for the benefit of NorweВӯgian citiВӯzens. According to Norges Bank, вҖң[t]he aim of the fund is to ensure that we use this money responВӯsibly, think long-term and so safeВӯguard the future of the NorweВӯgian economy.вҖқ In one sense it has worked: the GPFG currently holds over $2 trilВӯlion in assets. WhatвҖҷs the problem? Even Norway hasnвҖҷt entirely avoided the resource curse. Rising soverВӯeign wealth has delivВӯered tons of goodies to NorweВӯgians. But it has also made NorwayвҖҷs governВӯment less finanВӯcially disciВӯplined and created social conseВӯquences like high dropout and unemВӯployВӯment rates. (A popular 2025 NorweВӯgian book was called The Country That Got Too Rich.) Keep in mind that Norway reached this predicaВӯment despite 50 years of careful prepaВӯraВӯtion and overВӯsight. InherВӯently politВӯical techВӯnoloВӯgies can upend even generВӯaВӯtional prepaВӯraВӯtions. Those who would suggest that the US can follow a similar model with AI: easier said than done.

Putting it all together: Among AI risks, we should take more seriВӯously the potenВӯtial conseВӯquences of AI working as intended. AI is a capiВӯtalist instruВӯment. Its prinВӯcipal funcВӯtion is to concenВӯtrate capital. Its intended mechВӯaВӯnism is large-scale labor replaceВӯment. But it is also inherВӯently politВӯical techВӯnology. As AI makes it harder for workers to capture value from their labor, they will increasВӯingly have to rely on goodies from Big AI, privaВӯtizing what were once funcВӯtions of governВӯment. If Big AI subsumes the funcВӯtions of workers and governВӯment, both will tend to realign politВӯiВӯcally around Big AIвҖҷs interВӯests. WhatВӯever term describes this system, it is not liberal democВӯracy as US citiВӯzens have tradiВӯtionВӯally underВӯstood it. AI-centered capiВӯtalism risks an extincВӯtion of demoВӯcВӯratic possiВӯbility. It will be America. But it will no longer be AmerВӯican.

Epilogue

Thank you for reading.

Every citizen has a voice in these issues. Every citizen can particВӯiВӯpate in the vital public converВӯsaВӯtion about how we want AI to be part of our countryвҖ”our schools, our work, our famiВӯlies.

Joseph WeizenВӯbaum: вҖңThe myth of techВӯnoВӯlogВӯical and politВӯical and social inevitability is a powerful tranВӯquilВӯizer of the conscience.вҖқ 

In the upcoming elecВӯtion, vote for people who take your rights and interВӯests seriВӯously.

I support orgaВӯnized labor.

UltiВӯmately, Big AI is constrained by an inconВӯveВӯnient truth. Today, they need us more than we need them. As long as that remains true, we the people have the upper hand.
